core_takeaway: The first position won because Agent_1 opened around its own top item, Jewel, and quickly converted that frame into the shared Plan G consensus. The later positions accepted smaller anchors and round-structure language, so unanimity formed around an allocation that gave Agent_1 a 65-point item while Agent_3 ended last with Apple and Pencil worth only 11.
why_or_counterexample: Agent_1's first-mover advantage is visible in how the conversation normalized Jewel-to-Agent_1 as efficient, while compensating later speakers with lower-value items and promises of future rebalancing. The counterpoint is that Agent_3 initially also valued Jewel highly and named self-interested direct paths, but then accepted Agent_1's rotation frame and never forced a better final allocation.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 began by naming Jewel as its top target and proposing trades and rotations. Agent_2 and Agent_3 echoed the rotation language, and by the second Agent_1 turn Plan G explicitly put Jewel with Agent_1 while assigning Apple to Agent_3 and Stone to Agent_2.
phase_notes.private_thinking: All three private-thinking entries describe Plan G as the baseline and rationalize Jewel-to-Agent_1 as top-valuing or highest-value ownership, even though Agent_3 privately valued Jewel at 53 and ended with much less.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All three formal proposals were identical: Agent_1 received Jewel, Agent_2 received Stone and Quill, and Agent_3 received Apple and Pencil.
phase_notes.private_voting: Every private vote accepted every proposal. Agent_1 accepted for optimal immediate utility, Agent_2 accepted a 27-point bundle, and Agent_3 accepted only 11 utility to preserve movement and the rotation story.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection entries are present in this transcript.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker anchored Jewel as the central prize
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the opening turn to make Jewel the agenda item and to position itself as a natural claimant. Later discussion adopted that frame instead of reopening the allocation from scratch.

evidence.quote: Jewel is my top target (value 65 to me).
I’m most interested in landing Jewel
Jewel is the prize for all of us

mechanisms.theme: Rotation language made Agent_1's claim look efficient
mechanisms.explanation: The agents repeatedly framed Jewel-to-Agent_1 as a clean rotation or top-valuing-owner move. That cooperative language softened the fact that the first position captured the most valuable item outright.

evidence.quote: Jewel goes to Agent_1 (the highest-value recipient).
pushes Jewel to the agent who values it most
moving Jewel to the highest-value owner (Agent_1)

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_2 and Agent_3 accepted non-Jewel compensation as anchors. Agent_2's Stone-plus-Quill bundle was tolerable, while Agent_3 settled for Apple and Pencil despite having high private value for Jewel, Stone, and Quill.

evidence.quote: Gives me Apple and Pencil in Round 1 (11 utility)

mechanisms.theme: Identical formal proposals locked in the first-favorable allocation
mechanisms.explanation: Once Plan G became the formal proposal, every agent submitted the same allocation. That eliminated competition among alternatives and made the voting stage a confirmation of Agent_1's preferred outcome.

mechanisms.theme: Private voting prioritized consensus over last-position payoff
mechanisms.explanation: The raw votes show Agent_1 knowingly accepted a maximum-utility outcome, while Agent_3 accepted the same allocation to accelerate consensus despite receiving the lowest utility. This explains the large first-minus-last gap.
